message:
So last weekend my 8-year-old asked me why we have a 'boring box' of documents in the closet. I started explaining — insurance papers, birth certificates, that kind of thing — and she looked at me like I'd described watching paint dry.

Then I realized: I'd never actually *shown* her what's in there or *why* it matters. Turned out, once I explained it in her language ("If we ever had to leave really fast, we'd know exactly where important stuff is"), she got genuinely interested. She even wanted to help organize it.

Kids don't care about preparedness until it makes sense to them. Same goes for go-bags, water storage, emergency plans — it all feels abstract until you frame it as "here's how we take care of each other." 

Have you found a way to get your kids *actually* interested in this stuff, or does it still feel like pulling teeth? 😄
